explanation_used:
1. **Opening statement**: This neuron strongly responds to text containing enumerations or lists of items (such as lists of food dishes like "Shrimp salad. Ceviche. Salmon banh mi. Smoked trout dip," named people like "Mandela, Pat Tillman, Billie Jean King," geographic locations like "Madrid, Calatayud, Soria and Segovia," or conceptual phrases like "rule of law, sustainable and transparent infrastructure investment, freedom of navigation").

2. **Domain coverage**: The pattern appears across diverse domains: cuisine (e.g., smoked and Asian-fusion foods), geography and international relations (e.g., Southeast Asian regions and ASEAN terminology), entertainment figures (e.g., Filipino celebrities Vice Ganda and Vic Sotto), and historical/personal names. It occurs in descriptive, mid-paragraph passages rather than headlines, and spans multiple languages (English, Polish, Romanian, Spanish) when list structures are present.

3. **Contrast with low-activation examples**: Low-activation texts are typically headlines, datelines, or short news snippets (e.g., "CHICAGO, Oct. 30, 2020 /PR" or "Pakistan: Death sentence for Christian youth on blasphemy charges") that lack enumerations. They are concise, often single-clause statements without multiple items separated by commas or periods. High-activation examples, even when fragmentary, consistently feature at least two listed elements, whether concrete nouns or proper names.

4. **Closing interpretation**: The neuron detects syntactic structures involving enumeration, where multiple entities or concepts are presented in sequence. This suggests a focus on list-like constructions that provide detailed, itemized information across semantic domains.

results.actual_text: the heroic test pilot Aleksei Chkalov; and Maria and Evdokia Vinogradova, hailed for their textile output. In the background is the
results.plain_text: Three paintings of proud Russians marching with unrestrained joy during what was the height of Stalin’s Purges have travelled to the UK for the first time.  The paintings by the leading Soviet realist Aleksandr Deineka have been lent to Tate Modern by Perm state art gallery for a London show exploring the visual history of Russia and the Soviet Union in the first half of the 20th century.  Almost all the objects in the show come from the remarkable collection of the late graphic designer David King, a prolific collector of Soviet-era posters, paintings, photographs, books and ephemera.  Tate Modern acquired the collection and are putting more than 250 items from it on display to mark the centenary of the October Revolution.  But the Perm loans add to the story. The Deineka paintings were studies for intended murals in the USSR pavilion at the 1937 World Fair in Paris.  “Everyone is happy, it is all sunny, it is all progress and industrialisation in the new society,” said co-curator Natalia Sidlina. “It was the image the Soviet Union was keen to project abroad but the reality was very grim indeed.”  The paintings are a mix of the imaginary and the real. Some of the people marching are identifiable and include Aleksei Stakhanov, leader of the Stakhanovite movement of workers and a man who set astonishing records for the amount of coal he mined; the heroic test pilot Aleksei Chkalov; and Maria and Evdokia Vinogradova, hailed for their textile output.  In the background is the spectacular and unrealised Palace of the Soviets, a building that was planned to be the tallest in the world, with Lenin on top.  The pavilion was designed to showcase the magnificent success of Stalin’s USSR, and people were fascinated. The USSR pavilion had 2 million visitors, far more than any other pavilion including Spain’s, which was exhibiting Picasso’s Guernica. It also won more prizes than any other country.  The terrible irony was that, at the same time, hundreds of thousands of Soviet citizens were being murdered in Stalin’s Purges.  In another room at Tate Modern is a large vitrine containing mugshots of political prisoners, many arrested and executed in the 1936-38 Moscow Trials, show trials against “Trotskyists”. They include two of Lenin’s closest allies, Lev Kamenev and Grigorii Zinoviev, as well as ordinary people simply in the wrong place at the wrong time. The photographs, from KGB files, were obtained by King in the 1990s after Boris Yeltsin opened up some of the archives.  In total King assembled a world-class Russian and Soviet collection with more than 250,000 items.  Sidlina recalled being sent by Tate to take a look at the collection. “I said I have about two days and David started laughing.” She entered his Islington home and it was everywhere. “There was not a single couch or armchair in the entire flat, every single square inch was covered in shelving which David had built himself.”  King’s collecting addiction began when, as art editor of the Sunday Times, he was despatched to Moscow in 1970 to find photographs of Trotsky. It was a hopeless task but he spent the next decades successfully scouring the world for unknown images of Trotsky.  He always wanted his collection to be made public and it was acquired by Tate in 2016, with highlights going on display in the new exhibition.  They include many posters celebrating Lenin and Stalin, including one made for the comparatively tiny Moksha ethnic group, people who number only a few thousand who have a language which is, for unknown reasons, very similar to Basque.  Other posters have stirring and alarming messages such as: ‘Emancipated Woman: Build Socialism!’ and ‘Don’t Chatter! Gossiping Borders on Treason.’  A particularly rare exhibit is Lenin’s poster proclaiming in 1917 the overthrow of the provisional government, one of just a few hundred which were pinned up around Petrograd declaring the Bolsheviks victorious.  • Red Star Over Russia: A Revolution in Visual Culture 1905-55 is at Tate Modern, 8 November-18 February 2018.

results.actual_text: What a wonderful world for jazz icon Tony Bennett, who celebrated his 90th
results.plain_text: What a wonderful world for jazz icon Tony Bennett, who celebrated his 90th birthday with friends way more famous than yours. Bennett, who became a nonagenarian in August, was honored with a two-hour special that aired on NBC Tuesday. Shot in September at Radio City Music Hall, the star-studded “Tony Bennett at 90: The Best Is Yet to Come” featured performances by Lady Gaga, Stevie Wonder, Bob Dylan, k.d. lang and Michael Bublé, among others. Saluting the singer whose career spans nearly seven decades, they sang renditions of Bennett’s classic hits including “The Lady Is a Tramp” to “I’ve Got the World on a String.”  Even Alec Baldwin, on break from skewering Donald Trump on “Saturday Night Live,” showed up to honor Bennett in his own special way. He sported a suit and prosthetic nose in a loving impersonation of the jazz great. Gaga, who collaborated with Bennett in 2014 for the jazz album “Cheek to Cheek,” showed off her tattoo inspired by the icon, before performing her second piece of the evening “La Vie en Rose.”  "You're my family, Tony," Gaga said. "You really changed my life." Follow me on Twitter @makedaeaster

results.actual_text: Review It doesn’t matter how many times you’ve seen the iconic film The Snow
results.plain_text: Review  It doesn’t matter how many times you’ve seen the iconic film The Snowman, it’s still just as magical. And having an incredible 26-piece live orchestra to accompany the screening of the timeless classic in Blackpool Tower Ballroom is just the icing on the Christmas cake. Based on the book by Raymond Briggs, the tale follows a young boy whose snow creation comes to life at midnight, taking him on an adventure to see Father Christmas. The orchestra’s performance of Howard Blake’s soundtrack was simply breathtaking while eleven-year-old Livs Ball, who attends Hambleton Primary Academy, mesmerised the audience with her angelic performance of Walking in the Air. It wasn’t only the wonderful animation and live music that warmed the hearts of the audience, which packed the ballroom up to the top balcony. The stunning show, by Carrot Productions, opened with a beautiful Christmas overture, featuring well known festive songs such as Rudolph the Red Nosed Reindeer and Jingle Bells. Then the audience was treated to the traditional tale of Cinderella. The narrator delivered a lively version of the rags to riches story, while young Blackpool singer Neve Thomas, 12, performed a lovely rendition of Close Your Eyes. Next up the double bass player took centre stage to give an alternative introduction to the orchestra family - from the “sideways pea shooter” (flute) to the “naked piano” (harp). And the orchestra’s performance of The Snowman’s Waltz filled the room with joy as younger members of the audience couldn’t resist joining the Snowman himself for a dance. It was a delight from start to finish; there’s simply no better way to start the countdown to Christmas.
